coredata.dc:title: Oxidization and aggregation of first generation poly(amidoamine) with an enhanced fluorescent performance
coredata.dc:creator: Kuai, Yong
Zhang, Changchang
Hao, Dule
Liu, Xiang
Ma, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Organic amines are apt to be oxidized in natural environment. Therefore, preparation of fluorescent materials derived from organic amines should think of the role of oxidized amines. However, this is neglected according to some reports. Herein, first generation poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM 1.0G) dendrimers with inherent weak photoluminescence was oxidized by ambient oxygen under heating of 180 °C for 60 min which brought an obvious enhancement of fluorescent quantum yield (QY). The oxidation was proved by both infrared and 1H nuclear magnetic resonance. Image of transmission electron microscope (TEM) also revealed the aggregation of PAMAM 1.0G after they were suffered from the lasting heating. The enhancement of QY was also observed when the mixture of aqueous solution of PAMAM 1.0G and citric acid were disposed under the same conditions. And the QY reached as high as 64.40%, which is much higher than that of the fluorescent materials derived from sole citric acid. TEM image also exhibited the aggregation of the product. The carboxyl of the aggregated particle could be activated by N-hydroxysuccinimide and could be bonded with bovine serum albumin realizing the fluorescent labeling of BSA finally, which was demonstrated by the image of gel electrophoresis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Poly(amido amine)
Oxidization
Aggregation
Fluorescent quantum yield
Labeling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Heating treatment on aqueous solution of the PAMAM dendrimers
Effect of water fraction in PAMAM 1.0G solution and oxidation of amino groups at the presence of H2O2 on the fluorescent performance
Heating treatment on the aqueous solution of PAMAM 1.0G at the present of citric acid
Fluorescent labeling on BSA with OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA
QY enhancement of the heated PAMAM 1.0G
TEM images of the aqueous solution of PAMAM 1.0G before and after a heating treatment
Successive enhancement of fluorescent QY of OA-PAMAM 1.0G

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province of China (No. 1508085MB31 ) and Student Research Training Program of AHUT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectra of PAMAM 1.0G (54.7 mg mL−1) and OA-PAMAM 1.0G (6.8 mg mL−1) in aqueous medium at different excitations. The inset corresponds to photograph of aqueous solution of OA-PAMAM 1.0G under irradiation of an ultraviolet lamp. (b) Fluorescence spectra (340 nm excitation) of PAMAM 1.0G (0.14 mg mL−1) under varied temperatures for 60 min (c) Fluorescent intensities of PAMAM 1.0G solutions with varied total volume and water fractions suffered from heating of 100 °C for 50 min.
IR spectra of PAMAM 1.0G and OA-PAMAM 1.0G corresponding to (a) and (b) respectively.

1H NMR spectra of PAMAM 1.0G and OA-PAMAM 1.0G obtained in d 6-DMSO. Numbered H atoms in the structural formula of PAMAM 1.0G correspond to the chemical shifts of the marked peaks (1 ~ 5). The asterisked peaks in PAMAM 1.0G and OA-PAMAM 1.0G are duo to the solvent of methanol (CH3OH) and absorbed H2O respectively.
TEM images of the aqueous solution of PAMAM 1.0G before and after heating treatment corresponding to (a) and (b) respectively.

Regressed lines (fix the intercept at 0) between integrated fluorescent intensity of the aqueous OA-PAMAM 1.0G and the corresponding absorbance. Line (a) corresponds to OA-PAMAM 1.0G obtained in ambient oxygen while (b) refer to OA-PAMAM 1.0G obtained in presence of H2O2. The slope for quinine sulfate in 0.50 M H2SO4 is 3.43615 × 107. Photographs (c) and (d) in the inset correspond to that of (b) and (a) respectively.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of aqueous solution of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA. (b) Regressed line between the integrated fluorescent intensity (340 nm excitation) and the absorbance. The slope of the line is also shown in (b) for calculating the fluorescent QY of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA.

Photographs of SDS-PAGE of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA-BSA in daylight (the upper part) and the corresponding fluorescent imaging irradiated with an ultraviolet lamp (the lower part). Strip (a) is a molecular weight marker. Strip (b1, c1) are the image of pure BSA with addition volumes of 5, 10 μL respectively. Strip (b2, c2) correspond the images of 5 and 10 μL OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA-BSA solutions respectively. Strip (b3, c3) are the image of physical mixture of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA and BSA.
Fluorescence QY of the different generation of PAMAM without and with a heating treatment at 180 °C for 60 min.

head.ce:title: Oxidization and aggregation of first generation poly(amidoamine) with an enhanced fluorescent performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organic amines are apt to be oxidized in natural environment. Therefore, preparation of fluorescent materials derived from organic amines should think of the role of oxidized amines. However, this is neglected according to some reports. Herein, first generation poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM 1.0G) dendrimers with inherent weak photoluminescence was oxidized by ambient oxygen under heating of 180 °C for 60 min which brought an obvious enhancement of fluorescent quantum yield (QY). The oxidation was proved by both infrared and 1H nuclear magnetic resonance. Image of transmission electron microscope (TEM) also revealed the aggregation of PAMAM 1.0G after they were suffered from the lasting heating. The enhancement of QY was also observed when the mixture of aqueous solution of PAMAM 1.0G and citric acid were disposed under the same conditions. And the QY reached as high as 64.40%, which is much higher than that of the fluorescent materials derived from sole citric acid. TEM image also exhibited the aggregation of the product. The carboxyl of the aggregated particle could be activated by N-hydroxysuccinimide and could be bonded with bovine serum albumin realizing the fluorescent labeling of BSA finally, which was demonstrated by the image of gel electrophoresis.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic amines are utilized extensively in making fluorescent materials especially in preparation of fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The doped amines contributed a lot for the enhancement of fluorescent features of the CQDs. Nitrogen-doped CQDs were usually obtained by heating the mixture of carbohydrate and organic amine such as ethylenediamine [1], diethylenetriamine [2], linear-structured polyethyleneimine [3], ethanolamine [4], ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid [5] and branched polyethylenimine [6], etc. The produced CQDs performed a higher fluorescent quantum yield (QY) compared with that of sole carbohydrate. However, amino groups including tertiary amine are inclined to be oxidized by the surrounding oxygen, which will be beneficial for the enhancement of fluorescence. Imae and his coauthors reported that the fluorescent intensity of the solution of the forth generation of poly(amido amine) (PAMAM) increased a lot when exposed to air for 25 days [7]. Hence, when the fluorescent material derived from organic amine was planed to be prepared the oxidation of amine should be considered carefully. During the generation of nitrogen-doped CQDs amines might be oxidized by the surrounding oxygen. Perhaps the fluorescent performance might be contributed mainly by the oxidation of amine. These oxidized amines were hard to be removed completely in purification of the product due to their large molecular weight [3,6,8,9]. Hence the fluorescence of the CQDs should be attributed partly by the oxidized amines. Actually, some organic amines are prone to be oxidized under certain conditions such as aniline. Similarly, some fine chemicals such as dimethyl dodecyl amine oxide were prepared by oxidation of the corresponding amine [10]. Unfortunately this was omitted in discussing the chemical structure and the cause of QY enhancement of the nitrogen-CQDs [2–6,8,9,11].

Since PAMAM does not possess conventional chromophores such as some conjugated species in the molecule the fluorescence phenomenon of oxidized PAMAM aroused extensive interests [12,13]. Bard found that OH-terminated PAMAM could be oxidized by ammonium peroxydisulfate leading to photoluminescence with a high QY [12]. Imae reported that the fluorescent intensity of PAMAM 4.0G depended on temperature and its concentration [14]. And the higher temperature and concentration brought a more intensive fluorescent intensity. Additionally, the decreased pH value also helped the increase of the fluorescent intensity. This might be ascribed to the molecular rigidity of PAMAM after the tertiary amine combined with protons [13]. Summarily, oxidation and rigidity of PAMAM induced the enhanced photoluminescence.

Herein, we would demonstrate the oxidation and aggregation of PAMAM 1.0G which brought the obvious enhanced fluorescent performance when the aqueous solution was suffered from a lasting heating until the solvent water was removed completely. The fluorescent performance could be enhanced successively when doped with citric acid and the product dissolved in aqueous medium very well. That promoted potential application of the PAMAM dendrimers in more areas that needed a higher fluorescent QY.

The first to third generation of PAMAM dendrimers (PAMAM 1.0G ~ 3.0G) were prepared by Michael addition of ethylenediamine and methyl acrylate and successively by amidation with ethylenediamine according to the literature [15]. The prepared PAMAM dendrimers were sealed and stored in refrigerator for more than one month (4 ~ 6 °C). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), citric acid monohydrate (99.5%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (98.0%), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98.0%) and bovine serum albumin (BSA, ~66 kD) were purchased from Aladdin company (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used as received and the double-distilled water was used through all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heating treatment on aqueous solution of the PAMAM dendrimers
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous solution of PAMAM 1.0G (5.5 mL, 54.7 mg mL−1) was placed in an electric drying oven with forced convection at 180 °C for 60 min. On account of the high temperature the water component was evaporated gradually. Ultimately, yellow membrane on the bottle of the vessel was generated, which was collected and denoted with OA-PAMAM 1.0G thereafter. Fluorescent spectrum of OA-PAMAM 1.0G was measured and had a comparison with that of PAMAM 1.0G. Such a treatment was repeated under 60, 100, 140 °C respectively to inquire the effect of heating temperature on the fluorescent intensity of PAMAM 1.0G dendrimers.

In addition, PAMAM 0.5G, 1.5G, 2.0G, 2.5G and 3.0G dendrimers were disposed respectively with the same method (heating in the oven at 180 °C for 60 min) to investigate the changes of fluorescent QY before and after such a heating disposing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of water fraction in PAMAM 1.0G solution and oxidation of amino groups at the presence of H2O2 on the fluorescent performance
ce:section.ce:para: PAMAM 1.0G solutions of 2, 5, 10, 15 and 20 mL containing 416.2 mg PAMAM 1.0G (water fraction corresponds to 79.2%, 91.7%, 95.8%, 97.2% and 97.9% respectively) were placed in the drying oven simultaneously at 100 °C for 50 min. The samples of 2, 5, 10 mL were complete dried while the samples of 15 mL and 20 mL leaved about 1 and 3 mL respectively at the end of heating. Then a predetermined volume of water was added to obtain the solutions with the same total volume (8.00 mL). Such a solution of 5 μL was fetched and diluted with 3.00 mL water respectively to have a measurement of fluorescence spectrum. Their differences in fluorescent intensity were utilized to analyze the effect of the initial water fraction on fluorescent performance of OA-PAMAM 1.0G when they suffered from the lasting heating.

Aqueous solution of PAMAM 1.0G (5.5 mL, 54.7 mg mL−1) at the presence of 1 mL H2O2 was treated in the oven at 180 °C for 60 min for inquiring the effect of intensive oxidation on the QY of OA-PAMAM 1.0G.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heating treatment on the aqueous solution of PAMAM 1.0G at the present of citric acid
ce:section.ce:para: Weighted 151.7 mg citric acid and mixed with 5.5 mL aqueous solution of PAMAM 1.0G (54.7 mg mL−1). The added citric acid was dissolved entirely and the solution became acidic (pH = 5.5). Then it was put into the drying oven at 180 °C and was heated for 60 min for obtaining the citric acid-modified OA-PAMAM 1.0G (denoted with OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent labeling on BSA with OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA
ce:section.ce:para: Fetched 2.2 mg OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA and dissolved in 10 mL water followed with addition of 6.7 mg EDC. The reaction was kept at 30 °C for 30 min with a magnetic stirring. Then 3.8 mg NHS were involved in the reaction at 30 °C for 2 h. The product was purified with a dialysis tube (3000 kD) for 8 h for removing the impurities. During the dialysis the outside water was discarded and was refreshed three times. At last, 2 mL solutions from the dialysis tube were mixed with 2 mL BSA solutions (8.0 mg mL−1 in phosphate buffered solution) and the mixture was kept at 25 °C for 6 h for realizing the chemical attachment of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA to BSA (OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA-BSA). Then the mixture was loaded in a dialysis tube (7000 kD), which was placed in water for 8 h for farther purification. The outside solutions were removed and refreshed. Such a process was repeated for 3 times.

The obtained solution of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA-BSA in dialysis tube was employed in an experiment of sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE). Such solutions with 5 and 10 μL were dropped in the respective wells of the gel. The fluorescent labeling on BSA with OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA was demonstrated by the photographs irradiated under an ultraviolet lamp. The gel was also stained with coomassie brilliant blue and was taken a picture in daylight.

The images of transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN FEI Company) revealed the morphology of aqueous PAMAM 1.0G solution before and after heating at 180 °C for 60 min and provided a proof of the aggregation. Infrared (IR, Nicolet 380, Thermo Nicolet Corporation) and 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR, BRUKER 300, TMS as an internal standard) spectra provided explicit evidences of oxidation of PAMAM 1.0G dendrimers during the lasting heating. Fluorescence spectra (LS 45, PerkinElmer) were estimated to judge the enhancement of fluorescent features of OA-PAMAM 1.0G and OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA. Enhancement of the fluorescent intensity also provide a proof of oxidation of PAMAM 1.0G. During the measurement of fluorescent QY, quinine sulfate in 0.50 M H2SO4 solutions (QY = 55.00%) was chosen as a fluorescence standard to measure fluorescence QY. The QY was calculated according to the following formula.
QY
x
=
QY
Q
Slope
x
Slope
x
n
x
2
n
Q
2
where the subscript x and Q refer to the unknown sample and quinine sulfate respectively, Slope represents the slope of regressed line between integrated fluorescent intensity of the emission and absorbance, n is refractive index, nx/nQ equals 1.0 approximately in this case. The fluorescent labeling on BSA with OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA was demonstrated by the photographs of gel imaging (UVP GelMax Imager System, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: QY enhancement of the heated PAMAM 1.0G
ce:section.ce:para: When the aqueous solution of PAMAM 1.0G dendrimers was placed in the drying oven at 180 °C for 60 min, the product, OA-PAMAM 1.0G, emit a stronger fluorescence, which can easily be judged from inset of Fig. 1(a) while the aqueous solution of PAMAM 1.0G performs weak photoluminescence. Fluorescence spectra of Fig. 1(a) disclose that the fluorescence of OA-PAMAM 1.0G is much more intensive at a lower concentration than that of PAMAM 1.0G. The fluorescent intensity of OA-PAMAM 1.0G is 5-fold of PAMAM 1.0G approximately when excited by lights with 340 nm wavelengths. With the varied excitation of 320 ~ 380 nm, fluorescence emission of OA-PAMAM 1.0G locates 375 ~ 475 nm, which predicts the inhomogeneity of the OA-PAMAM 1.0G. Such a facile disposing brings an obvious fluorescent enhancement for the PAMAM dendrimer, which encourage us to exploit the causes of fluorescent enhancement.

We repeat the experiment with varied heating temperatures and water fractions in PAMAM 1.0G solutions to research their effects on the fluorescent intensity. Heating temperature affects the fluorescent intensity distinctly displayed in Fig. 1(b). With the rise of heating temperature fluorescent intensity enhances amazingly. Additionally, Fig. 1(c) reveals the effect of water fraction of PAMAM 1.0G solution on fluorescent intensity when the sample is heated at 100 °C for 50 min. It shows that the less content of water the more fluorescent intensity. At the end of the heating, the water in samples of 2, 5 and 10 mL are evaporated entirely while the samples of 15 and 20 mL leave small quantities of water. The fluorescent intensity differs obviously despite the samples contain the same amount of PAMAM 1.0G. The experiment tells us that the water fraction in PAMAM 1.0G solution influences the fluorescent intensity. It seems that temperature influences the enhancement of fluorescent intensity more intensive than that of the water fraction. These phenomena may be derived from the oxidation of PAMAM 1.0G by surrounding oxygen since any reactive component is not added in during the experiments and chemical structure of PAMAM 1.0G is stable enough to suffer from the lasting heating. The water in the samples may hinder the contact of PAMAM 1.0G and oxygen and therefore hold back the oxidation and produces weaker fluorescence. In other words, the samples with less water may be more apt to be oxidized producing more intensive fluorescence. Similarly, the increased temperature arouses more intensive oxidation of PAMAM 1.0G. Oxidation of PAMAM 1.0G may be the main cause of the enhancement of fluorescent intensity. Of course, we need more evidences to demonstrate this.

IR spectra of Fig. 2 show the slight changes between PAMAM 1.0G and OA-PAMAM 1.0G, which predicts that there are no massive structural changes during the heating treatment. The characteristic absorption peaks of –NH2 (3279 cm−1, stretching vibration of N–H) and –CONH– (1655 cm−1 of amide I and 1560 cm−1 of amide II) in OA-PAMAM 1.0G remains intact. In sensitive fingerprint regions, slight differences occur at 1253 cm−1, which ascribed to N-O stretching vibration of amine oxide [16]. In 1H NMR spectra of Fig. 3, chemical shifts of the numbered 1H of PAMAM 1.0 G marked in the inset with 1 ~ 5 correspond to NH 2 2.21, CH 2NH2 and CH 2CO 2.50, CH 2N 2.71 [17,18], CH 2NH 3.06, NHCO 8.27 ppm respectively. Chemical shifts of 1, 2 and 4 in OA-PAMAM 1.0G almost remain unchanged compared with those of PAMAM 1.0 G only the chemical shifts of 3 and 5 in OA-PAMAM 1.0G increase slightly. The shift of peak 3 should be arrived from oxidation of the tertiary amine. The polarity of oxygen atom in N-O results in the decline of electronic cloud density of H in 3. Hence the chemical shift increases a little. The shift of peak 5 should be attributed to intensive interactions of hydrogen bonds between two amides. Such interactions are reinforced once the solvent water is removed completely. Consequently, the spectra demonstrate the oxidation of PAMAM 1.0G dendrimer while the skeleton of the dendrimer remains unchanged when they are suffered from the intensive heating which consists with the aforementioned deduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM images of the aqueous solution of PAMAM 1.0G before and after a heating treatment
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images of Fig. 4 distinctly show that the heating treatment leads to aggregation of the PAMAM 1.0G. From Fig. 4(a), PAMAM 1.0G dendrimers dispersed uniformly in the aqueous medium. The branches of the molecule are spread freely. Nevertheless, the morphology of the PAMAM 1.0G varies tremendously shown in Fig. 4(b). The individual PAMAM 1.0G molecules disappear entirely while the nano-sized particles occur. This is derived from the aggregation of PAMAM 1.0G. The sizes of the aggregation are greater than 20 nm. What is more, porous architecture of the nano-sized particles can also be discerned (see the enlargement of a particle). The diameters of the pores are about 2 nm. The polar functional groups in PAMAM 1.0G act each other by hydrogen bonds or polar interactions, which arouses intertwining of the branched chains giving aggregation when the solvent is evaporated completely. Heating operation brings about tight molecular stacking and intensive molecular interaction, which is consistent to the analysis of chemical shifts of peak 5 in Fig. 3. Such intensive interactions restrict free rotation of sigma bonds in PAMAM 1.0G. It is the aggregation that leads to restriction of the free rotations of branched chains and the molecular rigidity. Hence the efficiency of non-radiation relaxation is inhibited, which causes the enhancement of photoluminescence. As stated before, the rigidity of PAMAM dendrimers arises with the decreased pH value, which leads to increased fluorescent intensity subsequently [13]. Proper solvent can also induce the aggregation of PAMAM and thus the fluorescent intensity is raised [19]. Other than these reported approaches, the convenient heating operation herein causes the wrapping of the branched chains of PAMAM 1.0G, which also increase the rigidity of OA-PAMAM 1.0G.

The enhanced QY of OA-PAMAM 1.0G is estimated quantitatively from Fig. 5(a). The QY, calculated from the slope of the regression line between integrated fluorescent intensity and absorbances, reaches up to 16.36%, which is far greater than that of PAMAM 1.0G themselves (QY = 5.68% according to our measurement). This value surpasses the QY of the branched dendrimer prepared by N,N´-methylene bisacrylamide and 1-(2-aminoethyl) piperazine [19] and some of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots [9,20].

According to the aforementioned analyses oxidation of environmental oxygen and the aggregation triggers the increase of the fluorescent QY in this case. With the improved temperature, the oxidation occurs more quickly and more completely. In addition, the decrease of total volume of PAMAM 1.0G solution with less water fraction results in faster oxidation similarly. In order to demonstrate effect of oxidation on fluorescence of PAMAM 1.0G, 1 mL H2O2 was mixed with the aqueous solution to create an intensive oxidation condition for inquiring QY change of OA-PAMAM 1.0G. The QY, calculated by slope of regression line of Fig. 5(b), reaches 22.65% and increases 6.29% compared with that of the one obtained in environmental oxygen. This reveals clearly that oxidation helps to improve fluorescent QY of OA-PAMAM 1.0G as can also be judged distinctly from comparison of Fig. 5(c) and (d). The oxidation does contribute to the augment of fluorescent QY. And this agrees with the results that oxygen in air [7] or liberated from ammonium persulfate [12] participates the oxidation of PAMAM dendrimers which is beneficial for the fluorescent emission.

It is the oxidation and aggregation that arouse the enhancement of fluorescent QY. Therefore, QY enhancement of some CQDs or carbon nanoparticles passivated with polyethyleneimine may be devoted by the oxidation and aggregation of the amine [2,3,6,8,9]. However, this is omitted in discussing the relationship between the fluorescent performance and the role of oxidized amine. During hydrothermal syntheses of such nanoparticles it is impossible that oxygen is isolated completely. Thus oxidation of the amine is unavoidable. Hence, more carefulness is needed in exploring the real reason for the photoluminescence of amino-contained materials.

Further experiments prove that this is a universal phenomenon which recorded in Table 1. When different generations of branched PAMAMA with terminal ester or amino groups (0.5G ~ 3.0G) is heated at 180 °C for 60 min the fluorescent QY of all are enhanced remarkably. These facts disclose that tertiary amines in the cores of PAMAM 0.5G, 1.5G, 2.5G can also be oxidized, which induce the enhancement of QY obviously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Successive enhancement of fluorescent QY of OA-PAMAM 1.0G
ce:section.ce:para: As stated above, QY of OA-PAMAM 1.0G has been raised a lot compared with that of PAMAM 1.0G though a convenient treatment of heating. Despite this, the QY still has a room of successive improvement. When PAMAM 1.0G solution is mixed with citric acid and suffered from a lasting heating of 60 min at 180 °C the QY of the product (OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA) arises successively. The fluorescent spectra and the QY of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA are revealed in Fig. 6. Fig. 6(a) exhibits the fluorescence emission of 447 nm under varied excitation with narrower half-peak width compared with that of OA-PAMAM 1.0 G shown in Fig. 1(a). This forecasts the more uniformity of the aggregations. The QY of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA reaches as high as 64.40% calculated from the slope of the regressed line shown in Fig. 6(b). Such a value suppresses many of synthesized organic fluorescence materials [21,22]. And the preparation way is much more convenient than complicated organic synthesis.

The carboxyl of citric acid combines with primary amino group of PAMAM 1.0G giving amide group. The resulted C–N in –NHCO– can not rotate freely due to the conjugation of –NHCO– species. Therefore the produced amide groups increase the rigidity which decreases the energy consumption when the excited electrons transmit back to the ground state. Fig. 7 displays the TEM image of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA, which exhibits the aggregation of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA clearly. This is similar with the morphology of OA-PAMAM 1.0G. Presumably, oxidation of the amino groups in PAMAM 1.0G by ambient oxygen is inevitable during the preparation of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA. Hence the tremendous enhancement of fluorescent QY of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA should be attributed to the increased molecular rigidity and the oxidation of PAMAM 1.0G component.

The carboxyl of OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA can be activated by NHS producing active NHS ester at the presence of EDC. Such an active ester can be replaced by primary amino groups [23] such as the one of BSA under very gentle conditions. Photographs of SDS-PAGE shown in Fig. 8 demonstrate the reaction. Apparently, the chemical bonding between OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA and BSA is realized which judged from strip (b2, c2). The labeled BSA performs fluorescent when compared with the unlabeled BSA (strip (b1, c1)). Strip (b3, c3) illustrates that OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA without activation by NHS can not be attached to BSA judged by none of fluorescence. This also proves the successful combination of citric acid and PAMAM 1.0G dendrimers. That illustrates OA-PAMAM 1.0G-CA with a high fluorescent QY has the potential application in labeling biomolecules.

Facile heating treatment for a period of time causes obvious enhancements of fluorescent QY of the multi-branched PAMAM dendrimers. The fundamental cause lies in the oxidation of amino groups and the intermolecular aggregation. The branched structure favors the intermolecular intertwining when the solvent is evaporated completely. The ambient oxygen involves in the oxidation of amines. For that reason we should consider the oxidation of amines carefully as beneficial factors for the fluorescent QY when we discuss the relationship between chemical structure and fluorescence mechanism.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province of China (No. 1508085MB31) and Student Research Training Program of AHUT.
